hub_subtitle: Inglês para Trabalho Remoto em Tecnologia & Quality Assurance

lesson.menu_title: Navigating Difficult Conversations -- Delivering Unwelcome News and Pushing Back Diplomatically
lesson.menu_desc: Two days before a release, Ana finds a critical bug and has to break the news: the ship date has to slip. She delivers the unwelcome news candidly but tactfully, without sugarcoating it, pushes back on the client's demand to ship anyway without overpromising, reassures him with a concrete plan, and reframes the setback -- we caught a serious problem before your customers did -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 20 -- Difficult Conversations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Ana Claudia Veraldi | Lesson 20 -- Difficult Conversations

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to tell someone about something difficult or unwelcome
a problem that delays or reverses progress
to make bad news sound more pleasant than it really is
honest and direct, even when the truth is uncomfortable
careful not to upset or offend when you say something difficult
to say something that makes someone feel less worried
to disagree with or resist a decision, politely but firmly
to describe a problem in a different, more constructive way

paras: Ana is a QA analyst on a fully remote team, and two days before a major release she found a critical bug. It was not a small one: it corrupted data on the payment screen, and shipping on Friday as promised was no longer safe. Ana knew she had to break the news to the client, Daniel, and she knew he would not be happy. In the past, she would have done one of two things: either sugarcoat the problem so it sounded smaller than it was, or blurt it out so bluntly that the client panicked. Neither worked. This time she prepared to be candid but tactful -- honest about the setback, but calm, and always with a plan.

On the call, Ana did not bury the bad news or pad it with excuses. She was candid: 'I'm afraid we've hit a setback. We found a critical bug on the payment screen, and I can't recommend shipping on Friday.' Daniel pushed for the original date anyway -- the marketing campaign was already booked. Ana understood the pressure, but she did not cave and she did not overpromise a fix she could not guarantee. She pushed back diplomatically: 'I completely understand the deadline matters, but I'd rather flag this now than ship something that corrupts customer data.' She stood her ground without raising her voice, acknowledging his concern while holding her position.

What made the difference was that Ana did not stop at the bad news. She reassured Daniel with a concrete plan: a fix by Wednesday, a full round of testing, and a new release on Thursday -- one day late, not a week. Then she reframed the setback. Instead of 'we failed to ship on time,' she framed it as 'we caught a serious problem before your customers did.' It was a delicate conversation, and she handled it with care. By the end, Daniel was not angry; he thanked her for being straight with him. The lesson was clear: delivering unwelcome news is not about softening the truth until it disappears, and it is not about being blunt. It is about being candid, staying diplomatic, and always pairing the problem with a plan.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B2 reading, difficult conversations at work

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Ana ships the release on Friday as promised and quietly fixes the bug afterward.
Ana delivers the unwelcome news candidly but tactfully, pushes back on the deadline without overpromising, and reassures Daniel with a concrete plan -- instead of sugarcoating it or being blunt.
The text explains how to design a secure payment screen from scratch.

gapfill.text: Notice that delivering unwelcome news is a set of MOVES, not a personality. To DELIVER the news gently, open with a softener: 'I'm afraid we've hit a setback...' To PUSH BACK without a fight, acknowledge and hold: 'I completely understand, but I can't recommend that.' To REFRAME, pair the problem with a path: 'What I can do is give you a fix by Wednesday.' The goal is not to hide the truth or to be blunt -- it is to be candid AND diplomatic at the same time.

bank.label: Useful phrases for difficult conversations
items: I'm afraid we've hit a setback -- the release will slip by a day.
To be candid with you, shipping on Friday is not safe.
I completely understand the deadline matters, but I can't recommend that.
I'd rather flag this now than ship something that corrupts customer data.
What I can do is give you a fix by Wednesday and a new date today.
I don't want to sugarcoat it, but I also don't want to overstate it.
Let me reassure you: we have a clear plan to fix this.
I hear you, and I'm going to stand by my recommendation on this.

lf.text: Four moves that carry a hard conversation. DELIVER the news gently with a softener ('I'm afraid...'). PUSH BACK by acknowledging first, then holding your line ('I understand, but...'). REFRAME by pairing the problem with a plan ('What I can do is...'). Stay CANDID -- honest without being blunt. You are not hiding the truth and you are not attacking; you are being straight AND kind.

items.situation: You found a bug that delays the release. Break the news to the client without sugarcoating it.
tips: I'm afraid we've hit a setback -- we found a critical bug, and I can't recommend shipping on Friday.
I want to be candid with you: the release needs to slip by a day.

items.situation: The client demands you ship on the original date anyway. Push back diplomatically.
tips: I completely understand the deadline matters, but I can't recommend shipping with this bug.
I'd rather flag this now than ship something that corrupts customer data.

items.situation: The client is anxious about the delay. Reassure them with a concrete plan.
tips: Let me reassure you -- here's the plan: a fix by Wednesday and a new release on Thursday.
We have this under control. One day late, not a week.

items.situation: A teammate asks you to promise a fix by tomorrow, but you are not sure it is possible. Avoid overpromising.
tips: I don't want to overpromise -- let me confirm the timeline before I commit to a date.
I'd rather give you a date I can keep than a date that sounds good today.

items.situation: You have to raise a delicate quality concern with a senior developer. Be candid but tactful.
tips: Can I flag something delicately? I think this change needs another round of testing.
To be candid, I'm not comfortable signing this off yet -- and here's why.

items.situation: The client reframes your delay as 'you failed.' Reframe it constructively and stand your ground.
tips: The way I see it, we caught a serious problem before your customers did.
I understand the frustration, but I'm going to stand by the recommendation to wait one day.

items: Round 1 -- Guided
Break the news that the release will slip. Open with a softener, name the setback, and stay candid but tactful. Speak from your notes.
Round 2 -- Push back
The client insists on the original date. Acknowledge the pressure, then hold your line diplomatically -- without overpromising a fix.
Round 3 -- Deliver the whole message
With no notes, break the news, push back on the deadline, reassure with a concrete plan, and reframe the setback as a quality win.
Curveball
The client calls your delay 'a failure.' Reframe it constructively and stand your ground calmly, without getting defensive.

answerkey.title: Reveal the difficult-conversation toolkit
list: Vocabulary: to break the news, setback, to sugarcoat, candid, tactful, to reassure, to push back, to overpromise, blunt, to reframe, to stand your ground, delicate.
Deliver the news: 'I'm afraid we've hit a setback -- I can't recommend shipping on Friday.'
Push back: 'I completely understand the deadline, but I'd rather flag this now.'
Reassure with a plan: 'What I can do is give you a fix by Wednesday and a new date today.'
Reframe: 'We caught a serious problem before your customers did.'; stay candid, not blunt.
Strategy: be candid AND diplomatic, never overpromise, and always pair the problem with a plan.
answerkey.note: Delivering unwelcome news is not softening the truth until it disappears, and it is not being blunt. It is being candid, staying diplomatic, and pairing the problem with a plan.

listenings.text: Hi Ana, it's Daniel. I wanted to leave you a quick note after our call about the release. Look, I won't pretend I was thrilled to hear it has to slip -- nobody likes moving a date with marketing already booked. But honestly, thank you for being straight with me. You didn't sugarcoat it and you didn't bury it in excuses. You told me exactly what the problem was, you told me you couldn't recommend shipping on Friday, and then you gave me a real plan: a fix by Wednesday and a new release on Thursday. That's the part I appreciate. You pushed back on my deadline, which I did not love in the moment, but you were right, and you never overpromised something you couldn't deliver. The way you put it stuck with me -- we caught a serious problem before our customers did. That's a good way to see it. So here is my one ask: could you send me a short written summary of the new timeline, the fix on Wednesday and the release on Thursday, so I can share it with marketing today? No rush, end of day is fine. Thanks again for handling this the way you did, Ana. Talk soon.

Hey Ana, it's Karen, your team lead. I listened back to your call with Daniel, and I want to give you a bit of coaching, because you handled a genuinely difficult conversation really well. The thing you did brilliantly was stay candid without being blunt. You delivered bad news -- a slipped release, two days out -- and you did not soften it into something meaningless, and you did not drop it like a bomb either. You were honest and you were kind at the same time, and that is exactly the balance we want. I also loved that you pushed back on the Friday deadline instead of just caving to keep him happy. A lot of people overpromise in that moment, and then they miss the new date too, and now they've lost trust twice. You didn't do that. Here is my one tip for next time, and it's a small one. Whenever you deliver bad news, try to hand over the plan in the very same breath. You did this, but lead with it even faster: 'here's the setback, and here's exactly what I'm going to do about it.' When the client hears a path forward at the same time as the problem, they stay calm. And keep reframing the way you did -- catching the bug before customers did is a win, not a failure. That's great instinct. Let's catch up on Friday.

extra_audio.text: Here is how Ana handled the difficult conversation with the client. First, she broke the news candidly: she told Daniel there was a critical bug and that she could not recommend shipping on Friday. Then, when Daniel pushed for the original date, she pushed back diplomatically and refused to overpromise a fix she could not guarantee. After that, she reassured him with a concrete plan: a fix by Wednesday and a new release on Thursday. Next, she reframed the setback, describing it as catching a serious problem before the customers did. Finally, Daniel thanked her for being candid and straight with him, and the delicate conversation ended well.
